coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of novel ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites with considerable photocatalytic performances in removal of organic pollutants under visible light
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.dc:creator: Zarezadeh, Somayeh
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Mousavi, Mitra
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, ternary ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots photocatalysts were successfully prepared by a simple strategy. Then, their characteristics such as structure, morphology, chemical, optical, textural, and photocatalytic performances were fully investigated. This study demonstrated that the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites showed remarkably increased photocatalytic performances compared with the ZnO and ZnO/BiOBr samples. In decolorization of RhB upon visible light, the highest activity was obtained when the volume of C-Dots was 0.25 mL, which was about 39.7 and 2.7 times premier than the ZnO and ZnO/BiOBr photocatalysts, respectively. In the ternary nanocomposites, the increased performance was mainly ascribed to the formed heterojunction between the counterparts, up conversion characteristics of C-Dots, and visible-light harvesting ability of BiOBr. The reactive species trapping experiments proved that
O2 − was the major species involved in the photocatalysis reaction. At last, the ternary nanocomposite displayed remarkable stability for recycling runs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots
Visible-light-driven photocatalyst
Ternary nanocomposites
Water pollutants

item-info.ce:copyright: The Society of Powder Technology Japan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for the ZnO, different ZnO/BiOBr samples, and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites.
(a) EDX spectra for the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples. (b–h) EDX mapping for the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite.
(a) SEM, (b) TEM, and (c) HRTEM images of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite.
XPS spectra for the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite: (a) Survey scan, and high-resolution spectra for: (b) Zn 2p, (c) Bi 2f, (d) Br 3d, (e) O 1s, and (f) C 1s.
(a) FT-IR spectra for the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples. (b) UV–vis DR spectra for the ZnO, different ZnO/BiOBr samples, and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites.

(a) Photodegradation of RhB over the ZnO, different ZnO/BiOBr samples, and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites. UV–vis spectra for degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation over the (b) ZnO, (c) ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and (d) ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples.

(a) The degradation rate constants of RhB over the ZnO, different ZnO/BiOBr samples, and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites. (b) PL spectra for the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples. (c) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption data for the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples.
A plausible mechanism for separation of electron-hole pairs in the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites.
The degradation rate constants of RhB over the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite in presence of various scavengers.
Cycling runs for degradation of RhB over the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite.
The degradation rate constants of RhB, MB, MO, and fuchsine over the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples under visible light.
Schematic illustration for fabrication of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites.
The textural properties of ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples.
Summary of the results for various nanocomposites based on ZnO for degradation of RhB.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of novel ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites with considerable photocatalytic performances in removal of organic pollutants under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, ternary ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots photocatalysts were successfully prepared by a simple strategy. Then, their characteristics such as structure, morphology, chemical, optical, textural, and photocatalytic performances were fully investigated. This study demonstrated that the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites showed remarkably increased photocatalytic performances compared with the ZnO and ZnO/BiOBr samples. In decolorization of RhB upon visible light, the highest activity was obtained when the volume of C-Dots was 0.25 mL, which was about 39.7 and 2.7 times premier than the ZnO and ZnO/BiOBr photocatalysts, respectively. In the ternary nanocomposites, the increased performance was mainly ascribed to the formed heterojunction between the counterparts, up conversion characteristics of C-Dots, and visible-light harvesting ability of BiOBr. The reactive species trapping experiments proved that
O2 − was the major species involved in the photocatalysis reaction. At last, the ternary nanocomposite displayed remarkable stability for recycling runs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the growth of various industries and human population has led to enhanced environmental pollution, as a major crisis. Among various treatment processes, heterogeneous photocatalysis has been considered as one of the most efficient technology to address environmental and energy emergencies [1–11]. In the attempts for relevant photocatalysts, ZnO has been regarded as an outstanding candidate, due to its facile synthesis strategies, low toxicity, and abundance [12–20]. Contrary to these characteristics, ZnO-based photocatalysis suffers from several shortcomings such as low visible-light absorption and high recombination rate of charge carriers, which restrict theirs widespread applications [21–25]. Among various strategies, coupling with narrow band gap semiconductors has been used as a promising option to enhance activity of ZnO, which can extend spectral range for visible-light absorption and facilitate separation of e−/h+ pairs [26–37].

Recently, much efforts have been focused on the bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, X = I, Br, and Cl), because of its suitable structural features and photocatalytic performance. In particular, p-type BiOBr has been reported as a photocatalyst due to its low band-gap, more visible-light utilization, high thermal stability, and high photocatalytic activity [38–41]. Moreover, carbon dots (denoted as C-Dots), as a new member of non-metal nanomaterials, achieved the attention of researchers with their exclusive properties like non-toxicity, photo stability, high biocompatibility, chemical inertness, low cost, and high electrical conductivity. Besides, C-Dots have excellent up-conversion characteristics, which able it to utilize entire spectrum of sunlight and the conjugated π structure of C-Dots comforts the adsorption of organic dyes and shows a high electron accepting and transporting properties. According to these properties, C-Dots can be applied as good cocatalysts for preparation of efficient photocatalysts, which boost visible-light harvesting ability and decrease recombination rate of the photogenerated charges [42–45].

Although, there are various reports about activation of ZnO by various semiconductor to improve its photocatalytic activity under visible light [27–34], there is no report about activation of ZnO through combination with C-Dots and BiOBr. Hence, in this paper, we report fabrication of ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots photocatalysts for the first time. These ternary nanocomposites were obtained by a facial procedure. Physicochemical properties of the fabricated samples were analyzed by XRD, TEM, HRTEM, SEM, XPS, EDX, FT-IR, UV–vis DRS, PL, and BET instruments. We also evaluated photodegradation of different dyes upon visible light. Furthermore, a probable mechanism for the highly promoted photocatalytic ability of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites was discussed. Besides, stability of the hybrid photocatalyst was evaluated through cyclic photocatalytic tests.
The detailed description for characterization methods included in the supporting information.

For synthesis of the ZnO powder, the suspension containing 5.220 g of Zn(NO3)2·4H2O and 150 mL of water was stirred at 25 °C. An aqueous solution of NaOH (5 M) was appended dropwisely into the suspension for adjusting pH value at 10. Then, the suspension was stayed under ultrasonic irradiation for 120 min. Finally, the suspension was filtered by centrifuge, washed, and dried to obtain white powder of ZnO [46].

To synthesize the ZnO/BiOBr (30%) sample, which 30% is the weight percentage of BiOBr, 0.35 g ZnO powder was dispersed ultrasonically in 150 mL of water. Then, 0.238 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was appended into the suspension and stirred for 120 min. Afterward, an solution containing 0.051 g NaBr and 20 mL water was appended drop by drop into the above suspension. After refluxing for 60 min, the obtained product was collected, washed by water and ethyl alcohol and dried.

C-Dots was prepared according to a procedure described previously [47]. For preparation of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst, in which 0.25 mL is volume of the added C-Dots suspension, 0.4 g of the as-prepared ZnO/BiOBr nanocomposite and 0.25 mL of the C-Dots were appended in 150 mL water and dispersed by ultrasound for 10 min. Then, the product was dried in oven. The other ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites with 0.125, 0.5, and 1 mL of the C-Dots solutions were synthesized by similar procedure (Scheme 1 ).

Photoactivity of the as-obtained nanocomposites was estimated by photocatalytic degradations of 250 mL for MO, MB, RhB (1.0 × 10−5 M), and fuchsine (0.77 × 10−5 M) solutions in a glassy reactor at 25 °C. Visible-light was provided by an LED (with 50 W) placed top of the reactor. Emission spectrum of this source has been reported elsewhere [48]. The source has high intensity in visible range without any irradiations in UV and IR ranges. Prior to irradiation, the mixture of 0.1 g of the photocatalysts and pollutants were kept at dark condition for 1 h to obtain adsorption-desorption equilibrium. At various illumination times, 3 mL of the solution was taken out from the vessel and concentrations of MO, RhB, fuchsine, and MB were monitored at wavelengths of 477, 553, 540, and 664 nm, respectively.

XRD instrument was applied to investigate the phase composition and crystal structure of the ZnO/BiOBr photocatalysts with varying BiOBr content, different composites of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots along with the pure ZnO. All the diffraction peak in the pure ZnO can be thoroughly ascribed to the wurtzite hexagonal phase, which coincides wholly with the standard data of ZnO (JCPDS No. 36-3411 card) [46]. In the ZnO/BiOBr nanocomposites, beside the peaks of ZnO, all characteristic peaks of BiOBr can be entirely assigned as the tetragonal structure of BiOBr (JCPDS No. 73-2061) [49]. Obviously, the tetragonal BiOBr peaks are gently intensified with an enhancing amount of BiOBr from 5 to 40% in the ZnO/BiOBr samples. This indicates that there is interaction between BiOBr and ZnO in the formed photocatalysts. For the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites, due to the low content and high dispersion of C-Dots over the ZnO/BiOBr nanocomposite, same XRD patterns were obtained for the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites [47] (see Fig. 1 ).

EDX analysis was applied to study the elemental composition of the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalysts. The EDX spectrum of pure ZnO contains Zn and O elements, whereas the ZnO/BiOBr (30%) hybrid shows the presence of Bi and Br in addition to the Zn and O elements. Moreover, EDX analysis indicated that the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite has mainly composed of Zn, Bi, O, Br, and C elements, which confirmed formation of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots hybrid. To more probe the detailed elemental distributions, EDX mappings of Zn, O, Bi, Br, and C elements over the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite were displayed in Fig. 2 (b)–(h). As seen, all elements were uniformly dispersed on the ternary nanocomposite. Obviously, the EDX mapping shows that BiOBr and C-Dots could be uniformly distributed on the whole surface of ZnO. Weight percentages of the elements in the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite were obtained from EDX mapping analysis and they are 30.4, 36.5, 15.2, 11.5, and 6.33% for Zn, O, Bi, Br, and C elements, respectively.

More information about morphology and microstructure of the as-synthesized ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst were studied by SEM, TEM, and HRTEM images. As seen in SEM and TEM images (Fig. 3 a and b), nanoparticles of BiOBr and C-Dots have been decorated on the spindle-like particles of ZnO. Fig. 3c exhibits HRTEM image of the ternary nanocomposite. The distinct lattice fringe proved high crystallinity of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite. The lattice spacing of 0.260, 0.280, and 0.320 nm were attributed to the (0 0 2) crystallographic plane of wurtzite phase ZnO [39], (0 1 2) plane of the tetragonal BiOBr [41], and (0 0 2) plane of the C-Dots [50], respectively. These results proved that particles of BiOBr and C-Dots have been successfully deposited on ZnO and heterojunctions have been formed in the ternary ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposite.

The chemical state and composition of the elements presented in the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite were investigated by XPS analysis. The XPS full spectrum (Fig. 4 a) demonstrated that the nanocomposite contains Zn, O, Bi, Br, and C elements. The two symmetric peaks located at 1044. 5 and 1021.40 eV in Fig. 4b are attributed to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, showing the existence of Zn2+ ions [39]. The observed peaks at 164.2 and 159.1 eV in Fig. 4c are belong to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2, which are characteristic of Bi3+ ions in BiOBr [51]. The peaks at 69.1 and 68.3 eV in Fig. 4d are attributed to Br 3d3/2 and Br 3d5/2, which are assigned to Br− anion in BiOBr [52]. So, the main chemical states of Zn, Bi, and Br elements in the nanocomposite are Zn2+, Bi3+, and Br− species [53]. It needs to be pointed out that Zn2+ XPS peaks shifted to higher binding energy; while, both the Bi3+ and Br− XPS peaks shifted to low binding energy in the nanocomposite compared to the pure ZnO and BiOBr, which can be ascribed to strong chemical bonding between BiOBr and ZnO counterparts [54,55]. Moreover, the broad O1s profiles (Fig. 4e) can be indexed to four symmetrical peaks at 529.8, 530.08, 532.2, and 534.0 eV. The peak at 529.8 eV can be associated to lattice oxygen in ZnO [51]. The peak at 530.08 eV is attributed to oxygen element presented in BiOBr [48]. In addition, binding energies of 532.2 and 534.0 eV are usually attributed to COC and CO groups, respectively [52]. From Fig. 4f, the main peaks of C1s were at about 285.0 and 289.2 eV, which are belong to the CC bond with sp2 orbital and CO bond, respectively [56]. These results further revealed coexistence of ZnO, BiOBr, and C-Dots counterparts in the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) sample.

FT-IR analysis of the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalysts were performed in the wavenumbers between 400 and 4000 cm−1, as presented in Fig. 5 a. In all photocatalysts, the bands observed at 1664 and 3486 cm−1 are indexed to the bending and stretching modes of the OH group from adsorbed water, respectively [27]. The ZnO peak is seen at 570 cm−1 [57]. The band at 519 cm−1 in the ZnO/BiOBr (30%) and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposites is attributed to the vibration peak of BiO bond [58]. For the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite, the observed bands at 1350–1460 cm−1 and 1600–1770 cm−1 are assigned to the CH2 and CO modes of C-Dots vibrations, respectively [45].

The optical feature of photocatalysts is very important parameter affecting photocatalytic activity, which is usually studied by spectrophotometers. From Fig. 5b, absorption range of the bare ZnO is mainly fallen in the ultraviolet area. After combining with BiOBr and C-Dots decoration, the absorption edges of the binary and ternary photocatalysts were moved to longer wavelengths. Also, absorption intensity of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots samples in the visible light region is larger that of the ZnO and ZnO/BiOBr photocatalysts. Thus, it is proved that BiOBr and CQDs have important effect on visible-light harvesting ability and generation of more charge carriers in this region.

The photocatalytic abilities of as-synthesized composites were studied by degradation of RhB in water. As seen from Fig. 6 a, there was no degradation of RhB after illumination for 6 h in the lack of photocatalyst, denoting that RhB was stable enough under visible light and its self-decomposition can be denied. Moreover, in the dark condition, the adsorption of the pollutant reached equilibrium, confirming that the presence of light is essential for complete removal of the pollutant. The elimination of RhB in the presence of pure ZnO was only about 33%. Evident enhancement of the degradation efficiency was obtained by the binary ZnO/BiOBr photocatalysts. As seen, the photocatalytic activity of the ZnO/BiOBr nanocomposites considerably depends on weight percentage of BiOBr and the ZnO/BiOBr (30%) nanocomposite showed the maximum photoactivity. With further loading of BiOBr, deposited particles were aggregated and larger particles of BiOBr were formed on the ZnO, leading to demolition of the formed heterojunction among ZnO and BiOBr [59]. Thus, separation of the photoinduced e−/h+ pairs could not happen efficiently, resulting in reduced photocatalytic activity. Moreover, photocatalytic performances of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots photocatalysts are remarkably larger than those of the ZnO/BiOBr photocatalysts. Between the ternary nanocomposites, the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite exhibited the highest activity, so that after 120 min of the light illumination, almost 100% of RhB was degraded over this nanocomposite. In contrary, excess C-Dots decreased photocatalytic activity of the ternary nanocomposite. This reduction may be ascribed to aggregation of C-Dot particles, conducting to increase recombination rate of the charges.

Fig. 6(b)–(d) display the dependence of optical absorbance for RhB during visible-light illumination in the presence of ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalysts. The intensity absorption peak for RhB at 553 nm gradually reduced with enhancing the light illumination and nearly disappeared after 120 min over the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite. While, only 59.3% and 24.7% of RhB were degraded by the ZnO/BiOBr (30%) and ZnO photocatalysts, respectively. Thus, the photocatalytic degradation results implied that the strategy of BiOBr and C-Dots deposition over ZnO is efficient in photocatalytic removal of RhB under visible light.

The observed rate constant (kobs ) for degradation of the selected pollutant over the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite was calculated to be 287 × 10−4 min−1, which surprisingly reached to about 39.7 and 2.7 folds as high as those of the ZnO and ZnO/BiOBr (30%) photocatalysts, respectively. The quantum efficiency of the process is largely investigated by the life time of the photoinduced charge carriers. Since, intensity of PL peak is related to the recombination rate of the electron/hole pairs, PL spectroscopy was used to study the yield of e−/h+ trapping, transfer, and migration. Fig. 7 b displays the PL spectra of the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples. As observed, the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite shows the lowest emission peak denoting that the separation rate is the highest compared with the pure ZnO and ZnO/BiOBr (30%) photocatalysts. This result is due to effective separation of the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs in the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite as a result of heterojunction construction between ZnO, BiOBr, and C-Dots counterparts of the ternary nanocomposite. In this part, nitrogen physical adsorption/desorption was done to investigate the textural properties of the photocatalysts. Fig. 7c presents N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms for the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples. As seen, all three samples showed type-IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loop [60], predicting the presence of mesoporous structure. The textural properties of the samples including surface area, pore size, and pore volume are displayed in Table 1 . The specific surface area of the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%) and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples are 5.17, 12.2, and 22.4 m2/g, respectively. The enhanced surface area of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite may endow the photocatalyst for higher photocatalytic activities.

Photogenerated reactive species including hydroxyl radical (
OH), superoxide anion radical (
O2 −), and hole (h+), are mainly involved in photocatalytic processes. To find the major species in the degradation process of rhodamine B by the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite, 2-propanol (2-PrOH), benzoquinone (BQ), and ammonium oxalate (AO), were utilized as
OH,
O2 −, and h+ scavengers, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9 , the photoactivity of ternary photocatalyst reduced after introducing BQ, AO, and 2-PrOH into the solution, with the inhibition order of BQ > AO > 2-PrOH, suggesting that
O2 − has a key duty in the photocatalytic reaction.

Durability and stability of the photocatalysts are important in practical applications. Long-term stability of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite was examined by recycling tests, which the results are shown in Fig. 10 . After each run, the nanocomposite was separated and washed with ethyl alcohol and dried for the next run. Obviously, there is no remarkably loss of the activity after five runs. Thus, the as-synthesized ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite is an impressive photocatalyst for removal of water pollutants with superior stability.

To further confirm the improved photoactivity of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposite, the ZnO, ZnO/BiOBr (30%), and ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalysts were used for photodegradation of cationic (MB and fuchsine) and anionic (MO) dyes. From Fig. 11 (a)–(d), it is inferred that the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite also exhibited more efficiency than the ZnO and ZnO/BiOBr (30%) powders in removal of MO, MB, and fuchsine pollutants. Photoactivity of this nanocomposite is 39.7, 9.61, 63.9, and 28.4 times premier than that of the ZnO and 2.69, 2.47, 2.15, and 2.27 folds as large as the ZnO/BiOBr (30%) for removals of RhB, MB, MO, and fuchsine, respectively. These results showed that the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) heteojunction possesses significantly increased photocatalytic performance due to high absorption of visible light, boosting of charges separation, and advanced textural properties.

In order to confirm the superior photocatalytic ability of the as-prepared ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite, the degradation efficiency of RhB by this nanocomposite and the photocatalysts based on ZnO under optimized experimental conditions has been compared and the results are presented in Table 2
[28,64–72]. As observed, the ternary ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst has the best activity in photocatalytic removal of RhB under visible light.

Briefly, we successfully synthesized novel ternary nanocomposites by combining C-Dots and BiOBr with ZnO for photocatalytic removals of different pollutants under visible light. Various characterization analyses demonstrated that there are intimate contacts among BiOBr, C-Dots, and ZnO. The ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite showed the highest photocatalytic ability for degradations of RhB, MB, MO, and fuchsine, exceeding that of the pure ZnO by a factor of 39.7, 9.61, 63.9, and 28.4, and the ZnO/BiOBr (30%) by a factor of 2.69, 2.47, 2.15, and 2.27, respectively. The improved photoactivity of the well-designed ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots nanocomposites was attributed to the up-conversion feature and electronic sink of C-Dots and high visible-light harvesting characteristics of BiOBr. In addition, the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite presented high stability and durability, which is important for the potential applications in environmental protection. This work provides a novel vision for the design of highly efficient photocatalysts based on ZnO for energy and environmental usages.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors genuinely acknowledged support of this research by University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Iran.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apt.2019.03.016.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
